reviewed: docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md (Accepted, post-82f608f rework) — pre-TDD implementation probe
method: Two-stage. (1) A 4-agent read-only probe drove real paths and a real filesystem to extract the slice's facts (typed-grant table, MC_HOME layout, migration surface, APFS/firmlink/case behaviour), then reconciled them against ADR-021. (2) Each alleged defect was assigned to an independent adversarial skeptic in ITS OWN GIT WORKTREE, instructed to REFUTE by implementing the design verbatim against the real mc/boundary code and running it — the two process changes this ledger asked for after the ADR-020 seam and the ADR-021 draft. Default verdict REFUTED absent an exercised witness.
result: 6 raised, 5 CONFIRMED (all major), 1 PARTIAL (minor). 0 refuted outright; one skeptic refuted its own assigned sub-claim with running code.
verdict_on_the_adr: ADR-021 is salvageable by patching, unlike its own predecessor draft — every defect is local to one decision and the predicate stays parametric. But it must NOT be TDD-ed as written: D10's TypedClaim is self-certifying (claim.Kind provably inert), D5 is unimplementable against D1's own types (no ownerUID parameter; the symlink leg cannot fire on a pre-resolved path), D10's nine-item permit list is not the closed set (~13 grants have no kind), and D4's broad_root has a MEASURED fail-open on macOS: /System/Volumes/Data is AUTHORIZED rw and reads the operator's real ~/.ssh private key. The park list is EMPTY — the unifying insight is that mc/boundary is pure and receives every root pre-resolved, so a missing host-path formula is the host planner's later slice, not a TDD blocker.
process_note: The worktree + drive-real-paths instruction earned its keep for the third consecutive time. F5 (the firmlink hole) is invisible to any amount of ADR reading: it required statting /System/Volumes/Data. F6's skeptic PREDICTED a fail-open in D1/D8's absent-member handling, wrote the test to prove it, and the test FAILED — refuting its own finding with running code. That is the decorrelated producer/judge principle working in the direction that is easy to fake and hard to do.

findings.summary: F2 is correct, and the counter-argument does not save the design. I implemented D1's signature and D10's predicate verbatim against the real mc/boundary code and drove it. The finding is structural, not stylistic: D1's JurisdictionInput has no member keyed by TypedKind (DeniedPaths, Home, MCHome, MCHomeClasses, HomeClassRoots, GatewaySecrets, RuntimeControls, OwnWorksource, OtherWorksources, GitControls, MissionControlRoots — none of them), so there is literally nothing in the Jurisdiction for `claim.Kind` to be looked up in. I could not write the lookup even when trying to. The only expression the predicate can evaluate is os.SameFile(id.Info, claim.Root.Info), where BOTH operands come from the caller. claim.Kind is dead data: a sweep of all ten kinds over one fixed source/root pair returns PERMIT for every kind. Consequently MC_HOME itself, MC_HOME/gateway/secrets, ~/.ssh, another Worksource's workspace, and HOME all PERMIT as typed sources claiming Kind=own-session while the very same source under the very same Jurisdiction DENIES as an ordinary mount (mount.denied_root / mount.cross_worksource). One bit of caller-supplied data flips deny to permit. Worse, the natural caller shape makes the check a tautology: a planner writes `dir := filepath.Join(base, scopeID, "home")` and passes it as both source and claim.Root, so the predicate degenerates to os.SameFile(x, x) — always true. I drove exactly the case ADR-017:399 names ("any sibling/ancestor/other identity is still denied") with the realistic bug: a scope-id mixup on /home/agent (ADR-017:681). The sibling scope's home mounts RW, silently. D10 therefore fails to deliver ADR-017:396-401 in the exact case ADR-017 spells out. Now the counter-argument, which is half right. TRUE: mc/boundary is pure policy called by trusted setuid code, the container model never reaches this seam, and D10's real value — Authorize always passing a ZERO claim so ordinary/profile mounts can never enter the typed path — is genuine and works. This is a correctness/defense-in-depth defect, not a live escalation. FALSE, and decisively: (1) purity does not forbid the fix — I built it and it resolves nothing new inside the package; TypedRoots arrives pre-resolved host-side, in exactly the same purity class as MCHomeClasses and RuntimeControls already are, so the contract:41-44 argument has no force; (2) D10's text does not merely under-describe, it asserts a property that is absent — "the claim is not trusted, it is CHECKED" and "A typed source that claims a kind it does not own fails the identity comparison against that kind's authorized root" are both false, since Kind is never read and no such root exists in the Jurisdiction; (3) it makes D9/D11 vacuous on the typed path — the same source+claim returns PERMIT against a full jurisdiction and against an EMPTY one, so the Jurisdiction contributes nothing but the `resolved` flag, and the ADR's own "changed jurisdiction flips the verdict" test can never flip for a typed source, which is the entire population D11 was written to protect; (4) the ADR's own deny-side test, written exactly as its test list words it ("a typed source claiming a kind it does not own rejects"), goes GREEN on the defective implementation — it pins os.SameFile(a,b)==false, not the kind→root binding it claims to pin. That is precisely the failure mode the D1+D3+D4 review caught, in the mirror: the draft pinned only the deny side and shipped a dead product; this pins a deny side that passes for the wrong reason. Since typed sources bypass BOTH the allowlist and the union, D10 is their ONLY check — and as specified that sole check is supplied by the thing being checked. ADR-021's words overclaim. The ADR's own Consequences bullet 2 already presumes the fix ("it must be added as an explicit typed kind with its own authorized root"), so the registry is a thing the document assumes exists and never builds — the same shape of gap as the draft's "D5's per-class check".

findings.recommendation:
Fix ADR-021 before TDD. Five concrete edits, all small; the fix is proven working above.

1. D1 — move the kind→root binding into the Jurisdiction. Add to JurisdictionInput, in the PRE-RESOLVED block beside MCHomeClasses/RuntimeControls (same purity class; the host caller already owes this work per "What gets harder"):
       // The kind -> authorized-root binding. Pre-resolved host-side. A kind
       // absent from this map has no authorized root and denies.
       TypedRoots map[TypedKind][]ProtectedID
   Use a slice per kind, not a single ProtectedID: /mc/session and /mc/records/output are per-run-id, and ADR-017:682 grants four exact tool-cache roots. (The alternative — one root per kind, Jurisdiction constructed per-run — also works and is stricter; if chosen, say so explicitly, because it forces a kind per cache root.)

2. D1 — delete Root from TypedClaim. Once the Jurisdiction owns the binding, claim.Root is not the caller's to supply and its presence is the whole defect:
       // TypedClaim names the KIND a typed system source claims. The authorized
       // root for that kind lives in the Jurisdiction, never in the claim.
       type TypedClaim struct { Kind TypedKind }
   The zero value (KindNone) still means "no claim", so D1's zero-value law and Authorize's always-zero claim are unchanged.

3. D10 — rewrite the predicate row and the false sentences. The table's typed row becomes: "kind-specific confinement: claim.Kind is looked up in the Jurisdiction, and the source must be os.SameFile-identical to one of the roots THAT KIND authorizes there. A kind with no authorized root denies (fail-closed). Any sibling, ancestor, descendant, or other identity is denied." Then fix the closing paragraph: "A typed source that claims a kind it does not own fails the identity comparison against that kind's authorized root — the claim is not trusted, it is CHECKED" is only true after this change; keep the sentence, and add one line stating the mechanism (lookup in j, never a caller-supplied root), the way D1's zero-value law was made to name its mechanism rather than assert the property. That is the same lesson this ADR already learned once.

4. D9/D11 — add one line noting that TypedRoots is part of the drift surface: a typed root whose identity changed between plan and start is drift and removes the unstarted container. This is what currently cannot happen at all (probe B: full vs empty jurisdiction, identical verdict), and it is what makes D11 mean anything for typed sources.

5. Tests — the current list cannot see this defect; three additions that die on the old design and live on the fix:
   - Sibling scope-id (the killer, ADR-017:399's own case): under MC_HOME/state/worksources/, the own scope's home PERMITS and a SIBLING scope's home DENIES for Kind=own-state, with the planner deriving both source and root from one expression.
   - Kind inertness: hold one correct source fixed and sweep claim.Kind across every other kind — each must DENY. On the current design all ten PERMIT.
   - Jurisdiction-dependence: the same typed source+claim must flip PERMIT→DENY when TypedRoots changes. Today it cannot flip.
   Add to the planted-mutants list: "claim.Kind ignored / source compared against a caller-supplied root instead of the Jurisdiction's" — it must die with an exercised witness.

Also worth a line in the Status section, since this ADR documents its own rework: the defect is the same species as the one the review caught (a mechanism deferred to and never written — the draft pointed at "D5's per-class check"; D10 now points at "that kind's authorized root", which likewise does not exist in the document). Consequences bullet 2 already presumes the registry ("added as an explicit typed kind with its own authorized root"), which is the tell.

Edit ADR-021 in four places; do not start TDD on the current text.

1. **Replace the enumeration at :611-619 with a derivation rule (normative).** Strike the false claim "every one of ADR-017's typed grants" over a nine-item list. Write instead: "TypedKind's domain is exactly the set of host-source rows in ADR-017 Decision 6's destination table (:634-702), including the setup/landing tables at :686-702, which Decision 5:380-381 and :373 sweep in ('except each exact typed own-source grant in Decisions 4, 6, and 8'; 'trusted setup/landing'). Resident-materialized and image-rootfs rows are not binds and carry no kind."

2. **Add the missing kinds**, at minimum: task-root (:636), task-source (:637), task-git (:640), sealed-pack (:645), projection (:653/:697), correction-out (:674, RW Verifier), correction-in (:671), revision-in (:672), context-in (:673), record-input-output (:670), package-cache (:682, root `MC_HOME/cache/<worksource>` per spec:763), attachment-out (:668), operator-worksource (:655) and operator-artifact (:659), plus the setup/landing rows (:691-702). Because D10 requires exact `os.SameFile` identity, each *nested* bind (`/workspace/source`, `/workspace/git`) needs its own authorized root — a parent kind will not cover a child.

3. **Add the guard test that would have caught this**: a table-driven test that enumerates ADR-017 Decision 6's host-source rows and fails if any row has no TypedKind. Make a kindless row a *build-time* error, not a runtime rejection. This is the structural fix — another hand-enumeration will drift again.

4. **Correct two adjacent defects the probe exposed.** (a) D1's comment `// session | own-state | runtime-control | projection | …` is a *third*, mutually inconsistent list — "projection" appears there but not in the nine. Make it point at the derivation rule rather than sampling. (b) D1's `JurisdictionInput` has **no field carrying per-kind authorized roots**, so D10's "the exact root its claimed kind authorizes" has no source; the only field available is `TypedClaim.Root`, supplied by the caller, which would make the claim self-certifying and contradict D10's "the claim is not trusted, it is *checked*." I had to add `AuthorizedRoots map[TypedKind][]ProtectedID` to `JurisdictionInput` to make the permit side pass at all. Pin that field in D1.

Also correct Sharp Edge #2 (:563-569): it says a future grant "inside MC_HOME that is not one of the fifteen enumerated classes" must be added as a typed kind. That is backwards about the live failure — correction, revision, context, and cache are *inside* the fifteen classes (:377-379 names them) and *still* need kinds they lack today.

findings.summary: The core accusation is true and I could not refute it. ADR-021:611-619 lists nine typed grants and claims they are "every one of ADR-017's typed grants." They are not. ADR-017's Decision 6 destination table (:634-702) is the typed-grant set — Decision 5:380-381 exempts "each exact typed own-source grant in Decisions 4, 6, and 8," and the table at :634-702 sits inside Decision 6 (which opens at :611), with :373 naming "trusted setup/landing" in Decision 5's own protected bullet. At least a dozen host-bind rows in that table fall inside protected classes and have no kind among the nine: /mc/records/correction (:674), the three record-input rows (:671-673), /mc/records/inputs/outputs/... (:670), the four package caches (:682, root MC_HOME/cache/<worksource> per spec:763), /mc/private/attachments/out (:668), the .mission-control task/projection roots behind /workspace, /workspace/source, /workspace/git (:636-645), and the whole setup/landing table (:686-702). I implemented D10 verbatim and drove them: all reject, and D10's own exact-identity rule ("any sibling is denied") also blocks the obvious workaround of reusing a listed kind — attachments/out cannot borrow attachment-in, another run's output cannot borrow output, the cache cannot borrow own-state. Meanwhile the ADR's own acceptance test — the nine — passes green. That is the review's own fatal pattern reproduced exactly: a green suite over a dead product, this time because the permit side is pinned for 9 of ~20 grants instead of 0 of 7. Sharp Edge #2 does not rescue it: it anticipates future grants *outside* the fifteen enumerated classes, but :377-379 literally names correction, revision, context, and cache among those fifteen, so these are present grants inside enumerated classes that still lack kinds. Two of the accusation's named consequences are REFUTED and should not be repeated: "no run writes output" is wrong (/mc/records/output IS listed and plans), and "Codex never launches" is wrong (/home/agent/.codex :683 and /home/agent/.claude :684 are both the selected canonical control directory, i.e. the runtime-control kind, which IS listed). The accusation is also imprecise about the mechanism: D1 does not close the enum — it writes "session | own-state | runtime-control | projection | …" with an ellipsis, and "projection" appears there but not in the nine, so the ADR carries two mutually inconsistent partial lists and neither matches ADR-017. The defect is therefore not a literally closed enum but a false completeness claim presented as the acceptance criteria, which is what an implementer builds from. Real blast radius: the Verifier cannot write its correction, no role can read prior outputs/revisions/context, package caches never mount, Homie cannot publish attachments, and no standalone task can run.

Both legs land. (a) ownerUID is genuinely unavailable: D1's `ResolveJurisdiction(in JurisdictionInput)` has no ownerUID parameter, `JurisdictionInput` has no such field, and `ProtectedID.Info` yields only the ACTUAL owner uid with nothing to compare it against — so D5's "is not owned by ownerUID" leg cannot be written at all. I drove a real foreign-owned HOME (`/Library`, uid 0, operator uid 501) through D1's verbatim signature and it was ACCEPTED. (b) The symlink leg is dead against the threat D5 names, with one honest precision: it is not literally unreachable — it fires against a NON-conforming caller that skipped EvalSymlinks (i.e. it catches a caller bug, a D1 violation). But for every caller that obeys D1's "PRE-RESOLVED by the host caller" mandate it can never fire, because EvalSymlinks consumed the symlink before D5 ever saw the value; I verified this exhaustively across direct/one-link/link-to-link/intermediate-link spellings. The synthesis is what makes this major: I drove D5's own stated threat (":341 — a symlinked or foreign-owned HOME would silently relocate the entire ~/.ssh-class member set") through the conforming caller and it lands verbatim — $HOME symlinked at an attacker dir resolves cleanly, the protected ~/.ssh relocates to the attacker's, and the operator's real ~/.ssh holding an actual key is left OUT of the protected set. The two legs that could have caught it are exactly the two F3 alleges are broken; the surviving legs (not-a-dir, is-root) both pass a relocated HOME. So D5's promise that "injection relocates trust, it does not remove it" is, against D1's types, not implemented and not implementable. I attacked my own confirmation twice and both refutations failed: deriving ownerUID from `MCHome.Info` accepts a consistent foreign pair (it validates the caller against the caller's own claim — trust-laundering, the exact thing D5:337 forbids) and collapses when MCHome is zero-valued, rejecting a legitimate HOME; and rescuing the symlink leg via an EvalSymlinks-idempotence check only catches the caller bug, never the threat. This is also a green-suite-over-a-dead-check risk in the c120c5c pattern: the ADR's own test line requires "a HOME that is /, a symlink, foreign-owned, or absent is refused at construction", and two of those four CANNOT be made to pass against D1's types — an implementer would most likely satisfy them by feeding an unresolved symlink path into `Canonical` (a non-conforming caller), which passes green while pinning nothing.

findings.recommendation:
Both changes are required, and the witness proves neither alone suffices (the fix rejects the real threat via the symlink leg, because the attacker dir is owned by the operator's own uid — ownerUID alone would miss it; and raw-Home alone leaves the ownership leg dead).

EDIT 1 — D1's `JurisdictionInput`: move `Home` out of the pre-resolved block and make it a RAW string, exactly like `DeniedPaths`:

    type JurisdictionInput struct {
        // Operator-authored / environment-derived, RAW. ResolveJurisdiction
        // resolves these itself; a caller cannot pre-resolve what it is not
        // trusted to have resolved correctly.
        DeniedPaths []string
        Home        string // the operator's real HOME (D5). Injected, never read
                           // from $HOME here; resolved AND validated by
                           // ResolveJurisdiction, not by the caller.

        // MC-derived and spine-derived, PRE-RESOLVED by the host caller:
        MCHome              ProtectedID
        MCHomeClasses       []ProtectedID
        HomeClassRoots      []ProtectedID
        GatewaySecrets      []ProtectedID
        RuntimeControls     []ProtectedID
        OwnWorksource       WorksourceRoots
        OtherWorksources    []WorksourceRoots
        GitControls         []ProtectedID
        MissionControlRoots []ProtectedID

        resolved bool
    }

`ProtectedID` is UNCHANGED — it stays the internal representation of the resolved HOME inside `Jurisdiction` (D4's broad_root still needs the resolved ancestor chain) and the pre-resolved input type for every other member. Only Home's INPUT type changes.

EDIT 2 — D1's constructor signature gains the parameter its own cited precedent (identity.go:55) exists to carry:

    func ResolveJurisdiction(in JurisdictionInput, ownerUID int) (Jurisdiction, error)

Prefer the explicit parameter over a struct field: it matches `TrustPolicyFile`/`TrustHomeDir`, the compiler finds every D11 call site (D11 requires re-running at four of them), and it cannot be silently left at a zero value.

EDIT 3 — D5's prose. Replace "carries the operator's real HOME as an explicit, already-resolved value" with an explicit statement that injected and pre-resolved are ORTHOGONAL, and that "validated, not trusted" REQUIRES raw: the caller supplies the value (so `mc/boundary` still never reads `$HOME` — D5's actual purity requirement is preserved), but the package does not trust the caller's resolution, because EvalSymlinks destroys the only evidence the symlink leg can test. Add one line: a symlinked HOME is REFUSED, not resolved-through — matching TrustHomeDir's seam ("a symlink to an otherwise-trusted object is not itself trusted", identity.go:79-80).

EDIT 4 — D5 must add an explicit warning: do NOT reuse `TrustHomeDir`/`trustedOwnerMode` for HOME. Reuse the lstat seam only. `trustedOwnerMode`'s mode leg (`perm&0o077 != 0`, identity.go:93) rejects a stock macOS HOME (0750 here, verified) and would make `ResolveJurisdiction` refuse to construct on a normal account — every mount plan dead, the exact c120c5c failure shape. D5 mandates FOUR legs; a mode leg is not among them and must not be smuggled in via the cited precedent.

EDIT 5 — D1's purity rationale #3 currently says "The MC/spine-derived members arrive pre-resolved". Add the carve-out naming HOME alongside `DeniedPaths` as raw-by-necessity, so a future reader does not "restore" the pre-resolved form and silently re-kill the symlink leg. The existing sentence at D1:181-184 ("The input is mixed by necessity") already makes this argument for `DeniedPaths` — extend it to Home with the reason: `DeniedPaths` is raw because it may not exist; Home is raw because its raw spelling IS the evidence.

EDIT 6 — the test line. Current: "Injected HOME validation (D5): a HOME that is /, a symlink, foreign-owned, or absent is refused at construction, not at use." Two of those four cannot be made to pass against D1's types today, and the likely "fix" is a non-conforming caller that passes an unresolved symlink into `Canonical` — green, pinning nothing. Per this ADR's own lesson, pin the PERMIT side first: "a stock 0750 operator-owned HOME constructs successfully" (this is what catches the TrustHomeDir trap), THEN the four deny legs, THEN the end-to-end: "a $HOME symlinked at a same-uid-owned directory is refused, and the ~/.ssh-class roots do not relocate" — that last one is the only test that would have caught this finding, and note it must use a SAME-UID target or the ownership leg masks the symlink leg.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTE (adjacent, not F3): leg 4's "or any filesystem root" is not covered by `Canonical == filepath.Dir(Canonical)`, which catches only "/". Real roots like `/System/Volumes/VM` and `/dev` are missed (verified). OR the Dir-self test with an st_dev comparison against the parent.

findings.summary: F4 is real, but not for the reason alleged — and I tried hard to refute it. D5's four legs, implemented VERBATIM, are CORRECT: I ran them against the real /Users/vinchenkov (perm 0750, uid 501, ACL-carrying) and they ACCEPT it, while still refusing /, /Users, a symlinked HOME, a regular file, and an absent path. So the accusation's headline ("the obvious D5 implementation rejects every real macOS HOME") is FALSE for a verbatim reading; the design logic is sound and needs no redesign. What survives is worse than a wording nit and is unconditional. (1) D5's ONLY code citation, in the exact paragraph mandating HOME validation, is `identity.go:55, :68` — and :68 IS `func TrustHomeDir(path string, ownerUID int) error`. The ADR points the implementer at the one function that kills the product, never names it, and never says "no mode leg". TrustHomeDir implements 3 of D5's 4 legs and adds a fatal 4th (perm&0o077); D1 hands the implementer BOTH a `Home` and an `MCHome` field, one of which legitimately wants TrustHomeDir. Driven: ResolveJurisdiction-via-TrustHomeDir refuses the real HOME, and D11 re-runs ResolveJurisdiction at plan, pre-create, and pre-start — so NO mount can ever be planned on this machine. (2) The suite is structurally incapable of catching it. The ADR's D5 test line (:629-630) pins FOUR deny cases and ZERO permit cases; I implemented that list verbatim and it went 4/4 GREEN against the broken variant, plus the broad_root test green over a 0700 fixture — 5/5 green, dead product. Worse, two of the four ("/" and "/Users") pass for the WRONG REASON: they exit on the mode leg ("mode 0755 grants group or other access"), never exercising the root/owner legs they claim to test. Every HOME in the package is a 0700 temp dir (identity_test.go:36-45, the only mkdir helper, used at 0o700 in ~15 places), and identity_test.go:118 already pins 0o750 as a REJECT. This is verbatim the shape the ADR-021 review itself caught and wrote a rule against at :616-618 ("pin the permit side first, because pinning only the deny side is exactly how the first draft's fatal defect stayed invisible") — the ADR applied that rule to D10's typed grants and failed to apply it to D5's HOME, one decision over. On the ACL: it does NOT matter today and is a red herring for the kill — the real HOME's ACE is `group:everyone deny delete`, a DENY ace, while the obligation is "any ALLOW ace granting a non-owner rejects", and the leg is vacuous anyway (TrustHomeDir accepts a 0700 dir carrying `group:everyone allow list,search`). But D5 must explicitly NOT acquire it: it is an open tracked obligation (PROGRESS.md:81, :1400-1404) that will land in `trustedOwnerMode`, so any D5 that routes through the Trust* seam silently inherits an ACL check its four legs never authorized, killing managed/network HOMEs later on someone else's machine.

findings.recommendation:
Do NOT redesign D5 — its four legs are correct as written and are proven to accept the real HOME. Make three surgical ADR-021 edits before TDD, plus one code rule.

1. D5 prose — disarm the landmine the ADR itself cites. Add: "This is NOT `TrustHomeDir` (identity.go:68). That function is MC_HOME's seam: MC creates MC_HOME itself at 0700, so it enforces `perm&0o077 == 0`. The operator's real HOME is 0750 on stock macOS and 0755 elsewhere — its mode is not MC's business. D5 has FOUR legs and NO mode leg. HOME validation must be its own function and must NOT route through `trustedOwnerMode` (identity.go:92-104)."

2. D5 citation — fix it. `identity.go:68` is TrustHomeDir's own signature, i.e. D5's only code pointer aims at the function that kills the product. Cite `identity.go:55` alone, or annotate: "(`identity.go:55`, `:68` — cited for the ownerUID *signature* precedent ONLY, never for the validation body)."

3. D5 must explicitly REFUSE the ACL obligation. Add: "D5 does not acquire the pending macOS ACL leg (identity.go:52-54, PROGRESS.md:81). HOME's ACL is the operator's business exactly as HOME's mode is — the real HOME carries `group:everyone deny delete` today, and a managed/network HOME may carry allow ACEs. Routing HOME through the Trust* seam would silently inherit that check the day the obligation lands." This is precisely why leg-sharing with TrustHomeDir is wrong even though the ACL is vacuous today.

4. The implementation, leg by leg (new `validateHome(path string, ownerUID int) error`, verified working — see f4_d5_probe.go):
   L1 existence+kind: `os.Lstat` (never `os.Stat`); refuse on error; refuse if `!info.IsDir()`.
   L2 non-symlink: refuse if `info.Mode()&os.ModeSymlink != 0` — lstat so the final component is not followed.
   L3 ownership: `info.Sys().(*syscall.Stat_t)`; refuse if the assertion fails; refuse if `int(stat.Uid) != ownerUID`.
   L4 not a filesystem root: refuse if `filepath.Dir(clean) == clean`; refuse if `os.SameFile(info, parentInfo)`; refuse if `parentStat.Dev != stat.Dev` (a mounted volume root).
   NO mode leg. NO ACL leg.
   Slug: `mount.denied_root`, NOT `mount.allowlist_untrusted` — TrustHomeDir returns the latter, which is a fourth independent reason reuse is wrong (D4 already routes broad_root to `denied_root`).

5. Test list (:629-630) — apply the ADR's OWN :616-618 rule, which it applied to D10 and forgot here. Replace the deny-only line with: "**Injected HOME validation (D5) — the PERMIT side first**: the real `os.UserHomeDir()` at its real mode (0750 and ACL-carrying on the dev machine) is ACCEPTED by `ResolveJurisdiction`; 0755 and 0700 HOMEs are accepted. A HOME fixture built only by `t.TempDir()`+0o700 is FORBIDDEN for this test — it is what makes the suite structurally blind. THEN the deny side: `/`, a symlink, foreign-owned, and absent are refused at construction — and leg 4 must be exercised with `ownerUID=0`, because on a real machine `/` exits at the owner leg and the root leg never fires." Note that today two of the four deny cases pass on the mode leg, i.e. they assert nothing they claim to.

6. Add to the planted-mutant list (the e01a2af precedent): "HOME validation given a mode leg (`perm&0o077 != 0`) — must die against the real `os.UserHomeDir()`", and "HOME validation routed through `TrustHomeDir`". Both die only against a real-HOME permit test, which is exactly the point.

Three edits to ADR-021, all documentation-level; no code decision changes.

1. DELETE `MCHomeClasses []ProtectedID` from D1's `JurisdictionInput` (021:137), and strike D3's retention paragraph at 021:253-255 ("The enumeration is kept anyway, because it is not redundant in the descendant direction for D10's typed confinement..."). Witness G disproves that sentence: D10's predicate reads `TypedClaim.Root`, supplied by the resident's typed-mount planner, and never consults the belt. Witness F disproves any union value: 0 of 10 verdicts change. Also strike the belt bullet from the Tests section (021:588-589, "every one of the fifteen `MC_HOME` classes"). Keep `MCHome ProtectedID` — D3's whole-tree root is the whole mechanism and is correct.

2. In D3, replace the deleted paragraph with the reason the belt is not merely redundant but not transcribable: ADR-017:376-380's fifteen words are CLASS names, not directory names. Four have no MC_HOME directory at all — `config` is `config.toml` + `routing.md` (spec:707, :718); `landing` is ADR-017:699-702's no-agent effect class; `runtime-auth` is spec:816's `mc onboard` section / spec:554's health signal; `control` is ADR-017:382's runtime control dirs, already a separate union member. The rest are spelled inconsistently (`cache` singular at spec:763 vs `backups` plural at spec:777 and onboard.go:282; `context` singular at ADR-017:673 vs `corrections`/`revisions` plural at :671-672), so no mechanical pluralization is correct. This strengthens D3's existing drift argument — MC_HOME/runs (phase1b-contract.md:221), MC_HOME/egress-audit (ADR-018:378), and MC_HOME/deployment.uuid are already outside all fifteen, and the whole-tree rule already catches them.

3. FIX D1's prose, which is the one thing here with real teeth. "MC-derived and spine-derived, PRE-RESOLVED by the host caller" (021:132) and "Canonical string // the resolved path" (021:120) cannot both be honored with D8 bullet 3 ("Every other absent member ... is treated as present via the nearest-existing-ancestor rule, not skipped"). Witness A: 0 of 15 classes pre-resolve on a fresh deployment; `MissionControlRoots` and other-Worksources' artifact roots are absent just as routinely. State the encoding explicitly: for a member that may be absent, `ProtectedID.Canonical` carries the DECLARED cleaned path and `Info` is nil, and `ResolveJurisdiction` must not error on it. Add the law that makes this safe (witness E): Rejects' ANCESTOR branch must walk `P.Canonical` upward and stat the ancestors — it must never read `P.Info` — because that is the only direction that can fire for an absent P; the equality and descendant branches read `P.Info` and correctly yield false, since ResolveSource returns mount.source_missing for anything under an absent root.

4. Add one test to the D8 line of the Tests section: "an absent OTHER-Worksource artifact root still rejects its parent" (witness D/E). The current list only pins "a non-existent deny path resolves through its nearest existing ancestor" (021:631-632) — i.e. only DeniedPaths, the one member D1 already types as raw `[]string`. Nothing pins D8 bullet 3's generalization to the pre-resolved members, which is the half that D1's type actually strains.
findings.summary: Every factual claim in (a)-(f) is TRUE, but the defect they are marshalled to prove is not. (a) there is no MC_HOME/config directory — only config.toml + routing.md (spec:707, :718). (b) "landing" is ADR-017:699-702's effect class; grep finds no MC_HOME/landing anywhere in spec or ADRs. (c) "runtime-auth" appears only as an `mc onboard` section name (spec:816, :804) and a health-signal name (spec:554, :595). (d) pluralization is inconsistent in both directions, as alleged. (e) the enumeration is already incomplete — runs/, egress-audit/, deployment.uuid are in no class. (f) is understated if anything: I ran the REAL scaffoldOnboardHome and ZERO of the fifteen class words exist as MC_HOME children afterwards; only 4 of 15 exist even under charitable pluralization. So the belt does invent phantoms and does miss real roots. BUT: I implemented D1+D3+D4+D10 verbatim and ran the union predicate with and without the fifteen-class belt over every real, phantom, and unenumerated MC_HOME path — ZERO verdicts differ. The belt is INERT, because every phantom and every miss lives inside MC_HOME, which D3 protects whole. F6's own evidence is therefore evidence FOR D3, exactly as the task hypothesised for (e), and it applies to all six accusations, not just (e). I also tried hard to confirm the flagged D1/D8 hole and REFUTED it with running code: my own fail-open test FAILED. A nil fs.FileInfo is a SAFE encoding for an absent member — os.SameFile(nil,nil) returns false without panicking, and the ancestor walk (the only direction that can fire for an absent root) reads P.Canonical and stats P's EXISTING ancestors, never P.Info; the equality/descendant directions read P.Info but their correct answer for an absent P is already false, since ResolveSource returns mount.source_missing for anything under a non-existent root. What survives is minor and documentary: D1's "PRE-RESOLVED by the host caller" / "Canonical // the resolved path" is false for 15/15 classes and contradicts D8 bullet 3, and D3:254-255's stated reason for keeping the belt ("not redundant ... for D10's typed confinement") is factually wrong — D10 reads TypedClaim.Root and never consults the belt.

findings.summary: F5 is real, and the mitigation I was told to check does NOT exist. Three things are separately true and I ran all three. (1) The firmlink premise reproduces exactly: /Users and /System/Volumes/Data/Users are dev=16777231 ino=16962 — the same object — and HOME and /System/Volumes/Data/Users/vinchenkov are both ino=266855; filepath.EvalSymlinks normalizes neither, returning each path unchanged; AGENTS.md reads byte-identical through both routes. (2) The accusation's own concession holds: os.SameFile DOES catch aliases of HOME and /Users — /System/Volumes/Data/Users and /System/Volumes/Data/Users/vinchenkov both reject with mount.denied_root(broad_root), because they share an inode with a member of HOME's Dir-walk chain. (3) The decisive check the prior probe skipped: the compiled blocked floor rejects NONE of /System, /System/Volumes, /System/Volumes/Data, /System/Volumes/Data/Users — zero-value and constructed policy alike, all false. blocked.go:28-69 has no "system"/"volumes"/"data" component and no matching basename glob, and its own comment ("it never scans directory contents") is why: it classifies addresses, and /System/Volumes/Data is an innocent address. So the finding does not drop to minor. The gap is precise: /System/Volumes/Data is a real APFS mount point (/dev/disk3s5, "root data") that is NOT SameFile with anything in HOME's ancestor chain (ino 1152921500311879682 vs / at ino 2), yet every byte of HOME hangs beneath it. D4 computes broad_root from HOME's own filepath.Dir chain — {/Users/vinchenkov, /Users, /} — so neither the equal, descendant, nor ancestor direction can ever see it, and neither can any other union member's ancestor direction (I built the realistic union — MC_HOME + ~/.ssh + another Worksource — and it still slipped). Driven through the full Authorize pipeline in ADR-021's D6 order, using ADR-021's OWN required broad_root fixture (allowlist "/", which D6 mandates precisely so "/" reports denied_root instead of not_allowlisted), the source /System/Volumes/Data is AUTHORIZED RW, and reading through the grant returns 419 bytes of the operator's OpenSSH private key. This violates ADR-017:390 verbatim — "a source may not equal or be an ancestor of HOME" — and hands the container RW on the entire Data volume. It silently permits what must deny, so: major.

findings.recommendation:
Edit ADR-021 D4, and add two required tests. The one sentence that is wrong is D4:316 — "The rule is computed from HOME's own resolved ancestor chain." That is exactly the assumption the firmlink breaks: on macOS the ancestor chain is not unique, and filepath.Dir only ever walks the synthetic-root route.

1. REPLACE D4:313-316's paragraph. Keep the (correct) rejection of a hardcoded ("$HOME","/Users","/") list, then say: the rule is computed from HOME's resolved ancestor chain UNION the alias routes to HOME. An alias route is generated by the volume mount points: for each ancestor A of HOME (HOME included), statfs(A).f_mntonname is the mount point of the volume hosting A, and that mount point and its own ancestor chain are also ancestors of HOME by reachability even though no filepath.Dir walk from HOME reaches them. Add them to broad_root. On this machine statfs("/Users").f_mntonname == "/System/Volumes/Data", which adds /System/Volumes/Data, /System/Volumes, /System — precisely the three that leak today. Use statfs, NOT /usr/share/firmlinks parsing and NOT a hardcoded "/System/Volumes/Data": statfs is the kernel's own mount table, needs no macOS-specific literal, is correct for a HOME on an external volume or a non-/Users HOME, and degrades to a no-op on Linux (f_mntonname == the path's own chain member), so the rule stays one rule on both platforms. This is the same "identity, never strings" idiom D4 already mandates.

2. STATE THE SCOPE — the fix is NOT broad_root-only. My TestF5_FullUnionStillMisses proves every ancestorDirection member has the identical blind spot: /System/Volumes/Data slipped past MC_HOME, ~/.ssh, and another Worksource root all at once. So factor D4's ancestor direction through one helper — ancestorRoutes(P) returning P's Dir chain UNION each chain member's statfs mount-point chain — and have BOTH broad_root and the "S is an ancestor of P" mirror walk consume it. Note in D4 why the descendant/equal directions need no change: an alias of a chain member is caught free by os.SameFile (inode equality — /System/Volumes/Data/Users and /System/Volumes/Data/Users/vinchenkov both reject today, and /System/Volumes/Data/private is SameFile with /private). The gap is exactly the volume mount point itself and its ancestors, which are aliases of nothing in the chain.

3. FAIL CLOSED on statfs. A statfs error on any ancestor is ambiguity, and D8's "ambiguity denies" already governs it — ResolveJurisdiction must return an error rather than silently produce a short broad_root set. Say so in D4 and cite D8.

4. ADD TWO TESTS to the "broad_root (D4)" bullet at line 601-603, which today reads "$HOME, /Users, / reject" and would ship green over this defect:
   - macOS firmlink: with allowlist "/" and HOME=/Users/<u>, EACH of /System/Volumes/Data, /System/Volumes, /System rejects with mount.denied_root, alongside the existing /, /Users, $HOME cases. Guard with runtime.GOOS == "darwin".
   - the permit side in the same fixture: an allowlisted descendant of HOME (~/src/project) still AUTHORIZES after the alias-route expansion — the expansion must not swallow the mount the system exists to make. This is the same permit-first discipline that caught the D1+D3+D4 fatal defect; a deny-only test here passes trivially and proves nothing.
   Add a planted mutant to the line-638 list: "broad_root computed from filepath.Dir alone (no alias routes)" — it must die with /System/Volumes/Data as its witness.

5. CONSEQUENCES: add a sharp edge noting /System, /System/Volumes, and /System/Volumes/Data now reject as HOME ancestors, which is correct (they genuinely expose all of HOME) but will surprise anyone who reads them as "system paths, nothing to do with HOME".
